coredata.dc:title: Tripeptide and phenylboronic acid-functionalized eco-friendly carbon dots for dual sensing of dopamine and their bioimaging applications in SH-SY5Y cells
coredata.dc:creator: Sangubotla, Roopkumar
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description: Facile and convenient carbon dots (CDs) with excellent biocompatibility and strong fluorescence using eco-friendly sources are in high demand for sensing and bioimaging applications. In this study, we used a hydrothermal approach to synthesize highly biocompatible CDs (BCDs) from green fruit vegetables (bottle gourd). By employing the carbodiimide coupling method, these BCDs were functionalized with the tripeptide and phenylboronic acid ligands, glutathione and 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid, respectively. This method enhanced the sensing potential of the nanomaterial for the crucial neurotransmitter, dopamine (DA). These functionalized BCDs (FBCDs) exhibited significant excitation-dependent emission behavior. The BCDs and FBCDs displayed strong blue fluorescence, (λem = 460 nm) and (λem = 458 nm), under excitation wavelengths of 360 and 351 nm, respectively. The FBCDs exhibited an excellent fluorescence quenching response to DA in the wide range of concentrations between 0 and 25 μM and achieved a detection limit of 2.14 μM. The FBCDs were verified as a dual fluorescent probe by demonstrating in vitro detection of DA in SH-SY5Y (human neuroblastoma) cells. The FBCDs also enabled superior bioimaging in SH-SY5Y cells via multi-color emissions, including of blue, green, and red. The practicality of the proposed FBCDs was also investigated in human serum.

head.ce:title: Tripeptide and phenylboronic acid-functionalized eco-friendly carbon dots for dual sensing of dopamine and their bioimaging applications in SH-SY5Y cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do, 13120, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do, 13120, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Facile and convenient carbon dots (CDs) with excellent biocompatibility and strong fluorescence using eco-friendly sources are in high demand for sensing and bioimaging applications. In this study, we used a hydrothermal approach to synthesize highly biocompatible CDs (BCDs) from green fruit vegetables (bottle gourd). By employing the carbodiimide coupling method, these BCDs were functionalized with the tripeptide and phenylboronic acid ligands, glutathione and 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid, respectively. This method enhanced the sensing potential of the nanomaterial for the crucial neurotransmitter, dopamine (DA). These functionalized BCDs (FBCDs) exhibited significant excitation-dependent emission behavior. The BCDs and FBCDs displayed strong blue fluorescence, (λem = 460 nm) and (λem = 458 nm), under excitation wavelengths of 360 and 351 nm, respectively. The FBCDs exhibited an excellent fluorescence quenching response to DA in the wide range of concentrations between 0 and 25 μM and achieved a detection limit of 2.14 μM. The FBCDs were verified as a dual fluorescent probe by demonstrating in vitro detection of DA in SH-SY5Y (human neuroblastoma) cells. The FBCDs also enabled superior bioimaging in SH-SY5Y cells via multi-color emissions, including of blue, green, and red. The practicality of the proposed FBCDs was also investigated in human serum.

The novel functionalized carbon dots (FBCDs) were synthesized by using tripeptide (GSH) and phenylboronic acid (MPBA) as ligands for dopamine (DA) sensing. For this, EDC/NHS coupling chemistry was adopted using eco-friendly CDs (BCDs) i.e., bottle gourd as a green fruit vegetable. The BCDs and FBCDs have shown strong blue fluorescence with emission maxima of λem= 460 nm and λem= 458 nm in DI water, respectively. The FBCDs showed a suitable sensitivity with a detection limit of 2.14 μM in wide concentration window of DA (0-75 μM). The FBCDs enable a dual platform for DA sensing through in vitro and PL sensing approaches. The FBCDs have shown multi-color emission response (blue, green, and red emission) in SH-SY5Y cells.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a momentous type of physiological neurotransmitter among the compounds of substituted phenethylamines. It plays a multi-functional role in the central nervous system (CNS), cardiovascular system (CVS), human endocrine system, and cellular metabolism [1,2]. Optimal levels of DA are necessary for healthy human brain development. Unfortunately, abnormalities in their production or distribution lead to numerous life-threatening neuro-related disorders such as schizophrenia, epilepsy, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease [3–5]. So far, the preponderance of DA sensors has been successfully sophisticated by the principles of various scientific techniques. These include electrochemistry, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), chemiluminescence, capillary electrophoresis, colorimetry, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), ionic liquid-based ultrasound-assisted dispersive liquid-liquid extraction (IL-USA- DLLME), and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [6–15]. Enzyme-based DA sensors have also been designed, which rely on the mechanical approaches of fluorescence [16,17] and electrochemistry [18]. Effective usage of these sensors has been hindered because of expensive, time-consuming, and highly sophisticated laboratory setups.

To overcome these obstacles, fluorescent carbon nanomaterials have emerged as a desirable alternative, as they exhibit stable fluorescence, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. In particular, eco-friendly carbon dots (CDs) are highly attractive for use in the rapid detection of DA in physiological solutions and cells. These CDs are defined by impressive water dispersibility, promising photobleaching resistance, minimal toxicity, and engaging biocompatibility properties that provide sufficient benefit to supersede the usage of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [19–23]. CDs have a broad range of applications in the sensing of complex biomolecules, for example, DNA, RNA, and enzymes such as tyrosinase, alkaline phosphatase, hyaluronidase, cell surface proteins, hemoglobin, neurotransmitters, and other environmentally significant organic dyes [24–32]. To ensure superior fluorescence sensing abilities, a biocompatible precursor with a suitable synthesis method and proper functionalizing agents is mandatory. Much research has been devoted to the synthesis of environmentally friendly CDs using naturally available sources. These include gelatin, β-cyclodextrin, chitosan, lignin, sewage sludge, and curcumin [33–38]. In this regard, Lagenaria siceraria, the green vegetable known as the bottle gourd, has a myriad of carbohydrates (chitosan and lignin), steroids (campesterol and fucosterol), and triterpenoids (bryononic acid) that can be derived from its fruit portion. In particular, the fruit contains a lipotropic factor (choline) that may provide therapeutic benefits in the treatment of mental ailments [39]. Noteworthily, CDs from natural sources, such as watermelon peels, papaya, corn, and persimmon juices, have a narrow particle size distribution (~2–6 nm) [40–43]. This contributes to their successful application in live-cell imaging and labeling. In addition, surface functionalization and heteroatom doping of CDs substantially enhance PL properties; subsequently, they have been adopted for bioimaging investigations [44–48].

Over the last decade, diversified boronic acid [B(OH)2)]-functionalized CDs have been developed because of their specific sensing potential with 1,2-cis-diol-containing substances, such as DA and other monosaccharides, with which they form boronate esters [49,50]. Notably, the use of minimally or non-toxic chemicals as precursors for CDs and a broad linear concentration range are important considerations when evaluating the sensing performance of DA in aqueous solutions and cells. George and co-workers designed APBA-modified CD-based turn-on sensors for DA with high sensitivity [51]. Unfortunately, the preparation of the CDs included trace amounts of hazardous chemicals, such as concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and ammonium chloride (NH4Cl). Liu et al. developed APBA-functionalized AgInZnS QDs for the determination of DA [52]. However, major limitation has been the extensive combination of chemical reagents. For instance, Zhao et al. proposed a fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based sensor using tungsten disulfide QDs (WS2 QDs) for DA sensing [53], although the sensor has reported low sensitivity with a limited concentration window for DA.

Glutathione (GSH) is a crucial tripeptide that contributes thiol, amino, and carboxyl functional groups, thereby substantially improving the aqueous solubility of CDs [54–56]. In another study, we synthesized gold-naringin NCs using GSH as a stabilizing agent and investigated its intracellular and bioimaging applications [57]. Phenylboronic acid ligands, such as 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid (MPBA), have been used for the determination of DA. Palanisamy and coworkers have developed colorimetric and fluorescence sensors employing gold and silver nanoparticles as sensing probes and MPBA as a ligand for DA [58,59]. Although increased sensitivities have been observed, further in vitro studies are required to determine their true viability. Both GSH and MPBA ligands are paramount for the preparation of gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) because of their stabilizing properties. For example, Chen et al. designed reasonable AuNCs using these stabilizers for DA sensing [60]. However, these reports are also limited by a lack of in vitro sensing and bioimaging studies.

In this work, we proposed a CD-based fluorescent sensing probe for the detection of DA in aqueous solutions and SH-SY5Y cells with an adequate fluorescence quenching response. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , eco-friendly CDs were synthesized from the nutrient-rich bottle gourd, by employing hydrothermal treatment (biocompatible CDs [BCDs]). The resulting BCDs did not have the potential to detect DA. This may be due to the fact that the lack of heteroatoms on the surfaces of the BCDs highly restricts their sensing capabilities. Hence, a simplified EDC/NHS coupling reaction was adopted successfully to functionalize the BCDs (functionalized BCDs [FBCDs]). These FBCDs provided excellent detection of DA. Subsequently, the practicality of the FBCDs was evaluated in complex biological samples, such as human serum, which provides insight to the potential of the present sensing system.

l-glutathione (GSH), MPBA, N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC hydrochloride), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (sulfo-NHS), l-dopamine hydrochloride, human serum albumin (HSA), l-glutamate, epinephrine, serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine), and glycine (Gly) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (pH 7.4), HEPES (4-(2-Hydroxyethyl)piperazine-1-ethanesulfonic acid), (pH 7), and Tris (tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane) (pH 8) buffers were employed for sensing and bioimaging studies. The pH stability studies for BCDs and FBCDs were carried out using PBS (pH 7.4). Deionized (DI) water was used for all experiments, and all chemicals were utilized in the experiment without additional purification.

Freshly available green-colored bottle gourds were purchased from the local market and used to prepare juice by adding water and using a home mixer. Freshly prepared juice was placed in a Teflon autoclave (75 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 8 h in a hot air oven and then cooled to room temperature. Later, the dark brown colored solution was collected and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min and further dialyzed with a cellulose ester membrane (MWCO: 2 kDa) for 24 h. Finally, the BCDs were obtained from the supernatant and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further application. The plausible structures from the BCDs are illustrated in Fig. S1.

BCDs were functionalized using the precursors GSH and MPBA via the EDC/NHS-coupling reaction [50]. The BCDs (10 mL) were mixed with a PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing a mixture of 0.16 and 0.079 g of EDC and sulfo-NHS, respectively. After sonication for 15 min, 0.0593 g of GSH (5 mL) and 0.0452 g MPBA (20 mL) were added to the BCD solution and magnetically stirred for 24 h in the dark at room temperature. Finally, the obtained yellowish-brown solution was dialyzed through a cellulose ester membrane (MWCO: 2 kDa) for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of DA by FBCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The solutions containing 100 μL of DA (concentrations from 0 to 75 μM) and 400 μL of PBS were placed into cuvettes containing 100 μL of FBCDs (0.25 mg mL−1) and incubated for approximately 30 min. Finally, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a PL spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 351 nm.

Human neuroblastoma cell lines (SH-SY5Y) were cultured according to the guidelines provided by the American Type Culture Collection. For this, 1% penicillin-streptomycin with 15% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Dulbecco's Minimum Essential Medium (DMEM) was utilized. The cells were grown and incubated under conditions of 5% CO2 and 37 °C. Then, cells were sub-cultured after 80–90% confluency was attained.

SH-SY5Y cells were cultured using a six-well plate and later incubated for 24 h by maintaining 80–90% confluency under temperature and atmospheric conditions of 37 °C and 5% CO2, respectively. Immediately, the medium was discarded, and fresh medium was added using different concentrations of FBCDs (20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 μg mL−1), followed by incubation for 48 h. Then, the MTT reagent was added, and the cells were incubated for an additional 3 h. Finally, a microplate reader was used to record the absorbance at 570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro sensing of DA by FBCDs in SH-SY5Y cells
ce:section.ce:para: SH-SY5Y cells were seeded in a six-well plate using coverslips in the essential medium and incubated under 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the medium was removed, and Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS) solution was utilized to wash the cells and to remove excess residual medium. A solution containing 50 μg mL−1 of FBCDs was used as the control that was added to the cells. Then, various concentrations of DA (10, 50, and 75 μM) were added to the FBCDs and used to treat the cells, which were then incubated immediately at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 1 h. Finally, coverslips were washed three times using DPBS, and the corresponding cell images were obtained via fluorescent microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging studies in SH-SY5Y cells using FBCDs
ce:section.ce:para: SH-SY5Y cells were cultured on the coverslips in the six-well plate using an essential medium and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Then, the medium was discarded, and the cells were thoroughly washed using DPBS to remove residual medium. Then, the cells were incubated with the FBCDs at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 3 h. Later, coverslips were washed with DPBS three times, and the cell images were processed using a fluorescence microscope.
All the results in this manuscript are indicated as the mean and standard deviation (±SD) of three individual experiments.

In the present study, we synthesized eco-friendly BCDs and functionalized them with the sulfur and boron-containing compounds, GSH and MPBA, using the EDC/NHS coupling reaction and detected DA using a simple fluorescence quenching mechanism. The BCDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C in a 75-mL Teflon-lined autoclave for 8 h using bottle gourd as a green source. Subsequently, BCDs were functionalized using GSH and MPBA assisted by carbodiimide-coupling agents (EDC/NHS). The prepared BCDs and FBCDs exhibited sufficient blue-colored emission under ultraviolet (UV) illumination. Then, the BCDs and FBCDs were centrifuged at 10, 000 rpm for 20 min, and the resulting supernatant solutions were refrigerated at 4 °C for use in the remaining experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterization of BCDs and FBCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the BCDs and FBCDs were studied in detail using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis. Fig. 1 a and b show the TEM images of BCDs and FBCDs, respectively which shows that the BCDs and FBCDs are spherical in shape and uniformly dispersed. However, a slight increase in the size was observed for the FBCDs, which is attributed to the successful functionalization of GSH and MPBA ligands on the surfaces of the BCDs (Fig. 1 b). The BCDs and FBCDs have showed the narrow particle size distribution in the range of 2–5 and 2–6 nm (Fig. 1 c and 1 f). The maximum portions of BCDs and FBCDs have the average sizes approximately 3.5 and 4.5 nm. Under high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) observations (Fig. 1 d and Fig. 1 e, insets), the images of BCDs and FBCDs did not show lattice fringes. Also the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns confirm the amorphous nature of the BCDs and FBCDs (Fig. 1 d and Fig. 1 e, insets). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 2 a and 2 b) of the BCDs and FBCDs display centrally positioned broad peaks at 22.1° and 22.15°, which correspond to the (002) facet of graphite [61]. Furthermore, the HRTEM (insets) and SAED patterns of the BCDs and FBCDs were well-correlated with the XRD patterns.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was used to study the chemical properties and functional groups of the synthesized BCDs and FBCDs. The FT-IR spectra of BCDs (i), MPBA (ii), GSH (iii), and FBCDs (iv) are shown in Fig. 3 a. The BCDs show N–H, CO, C–H, and –OH stretching vibrations at 1555, 1648, 2975, and 3342 cm−1, respectively (line i). MPBA shows characteristic peaks at 1012 and 1093 cm−1, which correspond to boronic acid (B–OH) and C–B groups, and the peaks at 2564 and 3253 cm−1 represent thiol (–SH) and O–H groups, respectively (line ii) [58]. The analysis of GSH indicated peaks at 1396, 1710, and 2524 cm−1, which denote C–N, CO, and thiol (–SH) groups, respectively (line iii). Interestingly, after functionalization with GSH through the EDC/NHS coupling reaction, FBCDs revealed newly formed peaks at 1654 and 1402 cm−1, assigned to the CO–NH bond [54]. After the coupling reaction, within the FBCDs, a remarkable peak shift was observed for the B–OH and C–B (1012 and 1093 cm−1, respectively) groups associated with the MPBA ligand (line ii) to 1027 and 1105 cm−1, respectively (line iv). This confirmed that MPBA molecules exist on the surfaces of the FBCDs [60]. Next, thiol (–SH) groups from MPBA and GSH were slightly shifted to 2526 cm−1 in FBCDs, suggesting that both GSH and MPBA ligands were successfully surface-functionalized on the FBCDs. Again, FT-IR spectra were investigated for DA alone and FBCDs treated with DA (Fig. S2). The FT-IR spectrum of DA revealed N–H, –OH (phenolic), C–H (aromatic), C–H (alkyl), CC (aromatic) stretching vibrations and N–H (bending) at 3338, 3200, 3035, 2937, 1519, and 1616 cm−1, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of FBCDs treated DA showed ester C–O stretching vibrations at 1030, 1163, 1210, 1268, and 1309 cm−1, respectively. Also, a broad band was observed at 3334 cm−1 in FBCDs treated DA, which may have attributed to the bonding of O–H stretching bands of DA and boronic acid groups [62,63].

TGA was performed to study the thermal stability characteristics of the BCDs and FBCDs. Fig. 3b shows the thermal decomposition of the BCDs and FBCDs that occurred in three stages at different temperatures ranging from 20 to 700 °C. The initial weight loss for BCDs was observed between 20 and 113.07 °C (2.707%), which was attributed to the loss of moisture content and hydroxyl (O–H) groups. The second weight loss occurred between 113.07 and 342.58 °C (34.33%). This was due to the decomposition of nitrogenous and oxygen-based functional groups, such as (N–H) and (CO). The final weight loss was observed between 342.58 and 700 °C (24.62%), which was assigned to the decomposition of aliphatic and aromatic carbons. Meanwhile, FBCDs also demonstrated thermal decomposition in three stages. The first weight loss was observed between 20 and 301.63 °C (17.63%), which was due to the loss of water molecules. In the second stage, weight loss was observed between 301.63 and 473.23 °C (4.099%), which was attributed to the decomposition of nitrogenous (C–N and –NH), oxygen-containing (CO and C–O), and boronic acid (B–(OH)2) functional groups. The final weight loss was observed between 473.23 and 700 °C (1.449%). The total weight losses for the BCDs and FBCDs were found to be 61.65% and 23.18‬%, respectively. This suggests that BCDs that undergo surface functionalization with MPBA and GSH to form FBCDs demonstrate excellent thermal stability properties. The boronic acid (B–OH) and thiol (–SH) functional groups present on the surfaces of the FBCDs may be responsible for this behavior [64,65]. The weight losses observed in the BCDs and FBCDs at different temperatures are listed in Table S1.

XPS studies were performed to analyze the surface elemental composition of the BCDs and FBCDs. Fig. 4 a (curve i) shows the XPS total survey spectra, which suggest that elements including C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s with binding energies of 285.08, 399.08, and 532.08 eV, respectively, are present on the surfaces of the BCDs. On the contrary, the spectra of the FBCDs demonstrate elements, such as C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, S 2p, and B 1s, with binding energies of 285.08, 399.08, 532.08, 162.98, and 189.98 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4a (curve ii). Interestingly, after functionalization of the BCDs, the peaks of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s were shifted. Furthermore, the deconvolutions of the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, S 2p, and B 1s are illustrated in Fig. 4b–f. The deconvolutions of the high-resolution spectra of C 1s displayed the appearance of three peaks corresponding to C–C, C–O–C, and O–CO groups at 284.88, 286.58, and 288.78 eV and 284.98, 286.98, and 289.18 eV, in BCDs and FBCDs, respectively (Fig. 4b, curve i and ii). The deconvolutions of the high-resolution spectra of N1s revealed two peaks responsible for the N–H and C3–N groups at 399.48 and 401.08 eV and 399.18 and 401.48 eV, in BCDs and FBCDs [36,37,66]. These results have shown the increased atomic weight % of N around 4.06 in FBCDs than that of 2.27 in BCDs. This increase in the N content of FBCDs has ascribed to the successful functionalization of the BCDs [43]. In addition, deconvolution of the high-resolution spectra of O1s revealed three peaks corresponding to CO, O–CO, and C–OH groups (Fig. 4d, curve i) with binding energies of 530.68, 531.88, 533.28, 533.88 eV and 530.78, 532.68, and 534.38 eV, in BCDs and FBCDs, respectively (Fig. 4d, curve ii). In the FBCDs, three characteristic peaks were observed in the high-resolution spectrum of S 2p, attributed to S 2p3/2, C–S–H, and S 2p1/2 groups, with binding energies of 162.98, 164.28, and 167.68 eV, respectively, which further confirms the successful functionalization of BCDs with GSH and MPBA (Fig. 4e). Interestingly, the high-resolution spectra of B1s had a binding energy of 189.98 eV, which verifies that the BCDs were surface-modified with MPBA (Fig. 4f). The appearance of C–S–H and the slight shift of the B–OH and C–B groups in the FBCDs correspond to the FT-IR results. Moreover, the XPS spectra of S 2p and B 1s in FBCDs suggest the functionalization of BCDs with the ligands, GSH and MPBA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of BCDs and FBCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characteristics of the BCDs and FBCDs were analyzed in DI water. Fig. 5 a demonstrates the combination of UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra. The BCDs showed π–π* and n–π* transitions at 217 and 283 nm, respectively (black line, Fig. 5a). In the FBCDs, these transitions were red-shifted to 230 and 327 nm, respectively (pink line, Fig. 5a). These transitions corresponded to the CC and CO bonds, respectively. Furthermore, tails of absorbance completely extended into the visible region [67,68]. Significantly, this red-shifting of the absorbance spectra suggests that the BCDs are well-modified with GSH and MPBA. Further, UV–Vis absorption behavior of FBCDs (Fig. S3 a) was studied by comparing with the absorption spectra of DA alone and FBCDs after the addition of DA in DI water. The absorption spectrum of DA showed the typical UV band around 254 and 280 nm. However, no significant absorption was observed in the visible region (Fig. S3 b) [69]. Further, FBCDs treated DA has showed two bands at 318 and 348 nm (Fig. S3 c). Interestingly, a gradual increase in the absorption was noticed in the wavelength region of 300–500 nm as compared to that of UV spectra of FBCDs. This suggests that FBCDs were probably involved in the formation of dopamine boroesters with DA. The BCDs exhibited bright blue-colored fluorescence with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 360 and 460 nm (black curves Fig. 5a). The FBCDs demonstrated strong blue-colored fluorescence with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 351 and 458 nm (pink curves, Fig. 5a). The inset [(i, ii) and (iii, iv)] images show the bright-field and UV illumination images of BCDs and FBCDs, respectively (Fig. 5a). Both BCDs and FBCDs showed excitation-dependent emission properties when excitation wavelengths were changed between 300 and 500 nm (Fig. 5b and c). The PL emission was red-shifted from 433 to 556 nm in BCDs and 407–554 nm in FBCDs corresponding to the respected excitation wavelengths (300–500). The intensity of the emission peak was gradually increased and reached a maximum at 460 nm under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm in BCDs. The emission peak intensity was also increased and attained a maximum at 458 nm with an excitation wavelength of 351 nm in FBCDs. These results are well supporting with the earlier synthesized blue-colored CDs [70–72]. The surface functional groups, such as [(CO, N–H, and –OH)], of BCDs and the newly formed groups, such as [(B–OH, C–B, and –SH)], in FBCDs may produce trap states containing divergent energy levels that are responsible for this property [73]. These results are consistent with previously reported blue-fluorescent CDs [42,54]. To investigate the optical properties in detail, quantum yields were measured for the BCDs and FBCDs which showed 9.8% and 12.2%, respectively. Noteworthy, small differences in the UV–vis absorption and PL excitation spectra and similar recombination dynamics may influence the PL emission spectra and nearly similar quantum yields in the BCDs and FBCDs [74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity studies of the BCDs and FBCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the BCDs and FBCDs was thoroughly studied via PL measurements with various concentrations of DA. First, the PL intensity of the BCDs was measured by adding DA concentrations ranging from 0 to 50 μM, as illustrated in Fig. S4. When the BCDs were treated with DA, a random fluorescence quenching response was observed. We attributed this response to the absence of heteroatoms and limited nitrogen content in the BCDs. Next, we studied the PL quenching efficiency of the FBCDs by adding different concentrations of DA (0–75 μM) using an excitation wavelength of 351 nm to investigate the sensing response towards DA (Fig. 6 a). Interestingly, FBCDs demonstrated excellent fluorescence quenching (>80%) when compared to BCDs. Moreover, a suitable liner relationship between (F0–F)/F0 and DA concentration was noticed in the concentration range of 0–25 μM with the correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.996) (Fig. 6b). Herein, F0 and F indicates the fluorescence intensity of FBCDs at 458 nm in the absence and presence of DA. The FBCDs achieved an LOD of 2.14 μM according to the equation 3σ/K. Here, σ and K are defined as the standard deviation and slope of the regression curve, respectively [75].

As in previous studies, major problems arose with regard to sensing DA, including co-existing interferants, such as alternative neurotransmitters, amino acids, metal ions, and other biomolecules. Therefore, the PL selectivity behavior of FBCDs was studied using DA, serotonin (5-HT), glutamic acid (L-GA), cysteine (Cys), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), and metal ions such as K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl−, and Zn2+ (Fig. 6c). For selectivity studies, DA concentration of 75 μM was taken, whereas a slightly higher concentration (100 μM) was used for the remaining interferants. The inset (A-M) shows the corresponding photographs of the fluorescence intensities of FBCDs and after the addition of other interferants under UV illumination (365 nm). To investigate the interference effects on the FBCDs in a detailed manner, we have selected other valuable potential interferants. These include common metal (Fe3+, Fe2+, and Cu2+) and heavy metal ions (Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, and Co2+), and other crucial pigments [Acid green 25 (AG 25), Congo red (CR), Crystal violet (CV), and Allura red AC (E129)]. FBCDs didn't show any fluorescence quenching that signifies their high selectivity (Fig. S5). Interestingly, PL quenching of more than 75% was observed in the FBCDs with DA, while no remarkable PL quenching was observed with the remaining interferants. This suggests that FBCDs exhibit sensing with high selectivity for DA relative to the existing potential interferants. These results are encouraging, as they suggest that synthesized FBCDs adequately detect DA. Table 1 presents the detailed comparison for the proposed FBCDs to the other analytical methods for the detection of DA, which indicates the better sensitivity over other [53,76–80]. Notably, the typical concentration ranges of DA for individuals aged over 17 years is from 0.34 to 31.39 μM in the blood or urine. Abnormal levels of DA outside of this concentration range may be responsible for a myriad of neurological diseases. For example, extreme levels and very negligible amounts of DA are involved in Schizophrenia and Parkinson's disease, respectively [81]. We assumed that FBCDs express increased sensitivity towards DA because of the existence of strong covalent bonding between FBCDs and GSH that preferentially stabilizes the FBCDs [54]. In addition, GSH may facilitate the stabilization of the boronic acid groups on the surfaces of FBCDs. This may lead to the improved quenching efficiency of the FBCDs for DA sensing as compared to the other interferants. These results demonstrate that the proposed FBCDs have sophisticated sensing abilities for DA. Further we investigated the sensitivity of the FBCDs in different biological buffers including HEPES and Tris. For this, we measured the PL intensity for the FBCDs after adding DA at different concentrations (10 and 75 μM). However, no linear fluorescence quenching was observed in FBCDs with Tris and HEPES buffers as shown in (Fig. S6 a and b). This fluorescence behavior might be depending on the ionic strength and pH condition of buffers.

The PL intensity of the FBCDs was measured using the typical salt, NaCl (0–2.0 mol L−1) at intervals of 0.2 mol L−1 (Fig. 6d). No significant PL quenching was observed in terms of PL intensities. These results suggest that FBCDs are highly resistant to ionic conditions and may have biological applications [82]. Furthermore, the PL intensity of the FBCDs was observed at different pH values (3−12) (Fig. S9). Interestingly, FBCDs demonstrated diverse PL behavior under acidic and basic pH conditions. A gradual increase in the PL intensity was observed under acidic conditions. This behavior may contribute to the protonation of hydroxyl (–OH) groups in an acidic environment. On the contrary, rapid PL quenching was observed in an alkaline medium. This response suggests the possible deprotonation of hydroxyl groups. Notably, the maximum PL intensity was observed in the neutral pH condition (pH 7.4), which is convenient for in vitro sensing and bioimaging studies of FBCDs. The PL intensity of the FBCDs was investigated using a PBS buffer of pH 7.4, which resulted in a maximum PL intensity of 90% for up to 120 days (Fig. S10). Moreover, photostability studies of FBCDs were conducted for 1 h continuously under a Xenon lamp, exhibiting no photobleaching and retention of the maximum PL intensity (~98%) (Fig. S11). These results suggest that the FBCDs are efficient fluorescent candidates to be applied to biolabeling and bioimaging studies [83].

The lifetimes of FBCDs before and after the addition of DA were investigated through fluorescence lifetime decay analysis, as illustrated in Fig. S12. Using the double exponential function, the fluorescence decay curves can be obtained from the following equation:

)

Here, D and τi represent the PL decay and lifetime, respectively, whereas ai is the corresponding pre-exponential factor [84]. The FBCDs exhibited a lifetime τ1 of 9.58 ns and lifetime τ2 of 0.026 ns. However, after the addition of DA, lifetimes were dramatically reduced to lifetime τ1 of 8.35 ns and lifetime τ2 of 0.024 ns. The plausible PL quenching of DA may be attributed to the formation of boroesters with DA. The cis-diol groups in DA readily interact with the boronic acid groups and form dopamine-boroesters on the surfaces of FBCDs. This facilitates the electron transfer from FBCDs to DA and results in the fluorescence quenching of FBCDs [52]. Based on the results of the FT-IR and UV–Vis studies, we believe that the possible fluorescence quenching of FBCDs was attributed to the boroester formation between FBCDs and DA. Furthermore, this quenching response was supported by the minimal occurrence of lone pair electrons on the nitrogen atom within the amino group of DA at neutral pH conditions [51]. More importantly, we imitated biological conditions for in vitro sensing of DA and bioimaging using a neutral pH of 7.4. The lifetime decay studies also suggesting the dynamic fluorescence quenching capacity of FBCDs. The possible working mechanism for PL quenching of FBCDs is shown in Scheme 2 .

The cytotoxicity of the FBCDs was studied using the MTT assay in SH-SY5Y cells. Moderate cytotoxic effects were observed after SH-SY5Y cells were incubated for 48 h with the FBCDs (Fig. S13). However, the viability of SH-SY5Y cells was gradually decreased with increasing concentrations of FBCDs. This suggests that the synthesized FBCDs show adequate cytotoxic effects on SH-SY5Y cells in a concentration-dependent manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular sensing studies of FBCDs in SH-SY5Y cells
ce:section.ce:para: FBCDs were explored as suitable fluorescent probes with intracellular sensing properties sensitive to DA in SH-SY5Y cells. Fig. 7 a shows the fluorescence images of FBCD-treated SH-SY5Y cells without the addition of DA, as a control. Moreover, the FBCDs were treated with varied concentrations of DA (10, 50, and 75 μM), and fluorescence images were obtained (Fig. 7b–d). The results showed that fluorescence intensities were reduced in SH-SY5Y cells depending on the concentration of DA. As the FBCDs showed blue-fluorescence with the desired emission wavelength of 458 nm, the blue-channel of the fluorescence microscope (excitation wavelength of 405 nm) is adequately sufficient to study intracellular sensing properties.

The FBCDs demonstrated multiple fluorescence emission responses in SH-SY5Y cells. The bioimaging potential of the FBCDs was studied under multiple excitation wavelengths: 405 (blue), 488 (green), and 543 nm (red), as illustrated in Fig. 8 a–d. A multi-color emission property was observed within the SH-SY5Y cells treated with FBCDs, displaying blue, green, and red-colored emissions. The FBCDs exhibited superior cellular uptake in SH-SY5Y cells within a 3-h incubation period. The bioimaging applications were also investigated in different buffers using FBCDs as fluorescent probes. The corresponding fluorescence microscopic images of SH-SY5Y cells under the above-mentioned excitation wavelengths by FBCDs in Tris and HEPES buffers are shown in Figs. S7 a-d and S8 a-d. More importantly, this multi-color emission response was ascribed to the excitation-dependent emission property of the FBCDs [85–87].

In this study, we synthesized a facile, green, and eco-friendly CD from the juice of the bottle gourd as a natural precursor. The resulting BCDs functionalized with tripeptide and phenylboronic acid (i.e., FBCDs) facilitated high sensitivity towards DA. The proposed FBCDs have a suitable detection limit of 2.14 μM for DA sensing in the linear range of 0–25 μM. The BCDs and FBCDs showed quantum yield of approximately 9.8% and 12.2%, respectively. The FBCDs demonstrated long-term PL intensity (approximately 90% for 4 months), superior photostability, and enormous biocompatibility properties. They also demonstrated adequate intracellular sensing of DA in SH-SY5Y cells. Bioimaging studies in SH-SY5Y cells confirmed that FBCDs have a multi-color emission response (blue, green, and red) under different excitation wavelengths. Therefore, FBCDs can be employed for the detection of DA in real samples such as human serum. In this context, a few of the previously considered shortcomings should be ruled out regarding FBCDs. For example, blue fluorescence is constrained for in vitro and in vivo models because of the interference developed by the complex composition of biological macromolecules. Moreover, FBCDs are limited by insufficient DA sensing. However, as demonstrated in this study, long-wavelength fluorescent FBCDs are efficiently sophisticated for DA sensing in both in vitro and in vivo modes.
